results.title: US Unveils $65.8bn 'Golden Fleet' Plan to Restore Maritime Dominance
results.body:
The United States Navy has unveiled an ambitious 30-year shipbuilding strategy aimed at dramatically expanding its fleet, reforming naval procurement and reviving America's maritime industrial base under the Trump administration's "Golden Fleet" initiative.

At the heart of the Fiscal Year 2027 Shipbuilding Plan is a proposed $65.8 billion investment in shipbuilding, including funding for 34 manned ships and five unmanned platforms in FY2027 alone.

Over the next five years, the Navy plans to acquire 122 ships and 63 unmanned platforms in what is shaping up to be one of the most aggressive naval expansion drives in modern American history.

Acting Secretary of the Navy, Hung Cao, described the programme as a critical response to mounting global security pressures and declining American maritime strength.

He said the United States was at a strategic turning point and needed urgent action to restore its dominance at sea.

The plan is built around three central goals: overhauling the way the Navy procures ships, strengthening maritime superiority through a mix of high-end and lower-cost vessels, and rebuilding the country's industrial shipbuilding capacity.

Officials have framed the "Golden Fleet" as a modern-day successor to Theodore Roosevelt's famed Great White Fleet, intended to reassert the United States as the world's leading naval power.

The Navy currently operates 291 battle force ships, significantly below the long-standing target of 355 vessels. The report delivers a stark assessment of the Navy's recent shipbuilding record, noting that while shipbuilding budgets have doubled over the past two decades, the fleet has failed to grow beyond its 2003 size.

A major pillar of the strategy is industrial reform. The Navy says only around 10 per cent of shipbuilding work is currently carried out through distributed production networks, but it aims to increase that figure to 50 per cent by expanding modular construction, digital ship design and nationwide supplier participation.

The FY2027-FY2031 procurement plan includes five Columbia-class ballistic missile submarines, 10 Virginia-class attack submarines, seven Arleigh Burke-class destroyers, four frigates, two amphibious assault ships, five San Antonio-class amphibious transport docks, 23 Medium Landing Ships, seven fleet oilers and five ocean surveillance vessels.

The Navy also plans to accelerate procurement of the future CVN 82 aircraft carrier by moving its acquisition timeline forward from FY2030 to FY2029.

Perhaps the most striking feature of the plan is the proposal for a new nuclear-powered battleship, designated BBGN. The vessel is expected to provide long-range strike capability, survivable command-and-control functions, enhanced power generation and expanded capacity for advanced weapons systems.

The Navy insists the battleship is not intended to replace destroyers, but rather to serve as a new class of high-end surface combatant capable of adding greater combat mass to the fleet.

Alongside these high-capability platforms, the Navy is also shifting attention towards a new generation of frigates designed to take over missions currently handled by destroyers, including convoy escort, anti-submarine warfare, maritime interdiction, homeland defence and counter-narcotics operations.

The plan also places growing emphasis on unmanned systems. The FY2027 budget includes three Medium Unmanned Surface Vessels, while the broader five-year programme seeks 47 such vessels alongside 16 Extra Large Unmanned Underwater Vehicles.

results.title: Defense Watch: Mythos, DARC, DARPA Plane, New SWO Boss, Startup Raises - Defense Daily
results.body:
Michael on Mythos. Emil Michael, the Pentagon's chief technology officer, said there is broad interest across the U.S. government in assessing Anthropic's new Mythos artificial intelligence-powered cyber security model, which he said is "independent" of the department's current effort to move on from using the technology firm's Claude AI model. "When you have a model that has cyber vulnerability capabilities where you can find those kind of [vulnerabilities] in a step function change way, that means you have to look at your own networks, the country's networks and find out what vulnerabilities they have so they can't be exploited," Michael told reporters on May 6 at the Pentagon. "The whole government has to look at what this model can do, not only [for] the government hardware and software infrastructure but to the private sector...so that we have the ability to patch [vulnerabilities] before adversaries get the same ability." President Donald Trump in February ordered the federal government to halt the use of Anthropic's products after the company said it wanted oversight safeguards for its Pentagon work.

...LUCAS Drones. Michael also said the Pentagon is working on how it may scale up production of the Low-Cost Unmanned Combat Attack System (LUCAS), used for the first time in combat as part of the U.S.' Operation Epic Fury campaign against Iran. The SpektreWorks-built LUCAS drones were reverse engineered from Iran's Shahed one-way attack systems. "So based on a real world field test where you're not just right on a range [but] you're actually trying it, you get data faster. That means the chances that we can put it on an order path where it becomes a program of record are much higher. And we're working on that right now," he said.

Oh, DARC Early. The Space Force said that it "has fast tracked the employment" of Northrop Grumman's Deep Space Advanced Radar Capability (DARC) that Australia, the United Kingdom and the U.S. are to field under the AUKUS agreement. The schedule has called for Australia to field DARC this year, the U.K. in 2028, and the U.S. in 2029. Last September, U.S. Space Command approved DARC for early use through "streamlining" test and evaluation and proving system readiness in the support of "joint and combined force objectives," according to Space Force's Combat Forces Command. The 20 Space Surveillance Squadron's Integrated Radar Operations Center at Eglin AFB, Fla. is conducting the early use of DARC, which is to provide "space domain awareness out to geosynchronous orbit and enable tactical timelines and multiple space control kill chains," according to Space Force.

X-Plane Flight. The Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), working with Northrop Grumman and the Air Force Research Laboratory, last week said it flew its unmanned X-Plane, the XRQ-73 hybrid-electric experimental aircraft at Edwards AFB, Calif., in April. The flight was part of DARPA's Series Hybrid Electric Propulsion AiRcraft Demonstration, dubbed SHEPARD. The 1,250-pound Group 3 unmanned aircraft system had been expected to fly in 2024 (Defense Daily, June 28, 2024). The agency said the flight is step toward demonstrating military utility of hybrid-electric propulsion, which could drive new aircraft designs.

Rocket Lab SBI. Rocket Lab on May 7 announced it is partnering with RTX's Raytheon for its participation in the Space Force's Space Based Interceptor (CBI) program. Last month, the government revealed 12 companies won Other Transaction Authority agreements in late 2025 and early 2026 to work on SBIs, including RTX. The companies did not specify what technologies or systems they are developing in the SBI work. Rocket Lab manufactures spacecraft and launch vehicles.

Classified York Awards. York Space Systems has won positions on multiple classified indefinite-delivery, indefinite-quantity contracts "across two mission areas critical to national security and defense," the Colorado-based satellite manufacturer and operator said last week. "These awards expand York's role in next-generation space and defense architectures." The company also said the work supports systems like the Golden Dome homeland missile defense program.

SWO Boss. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth on May 5 announced the nomination of Rear Adm. Joseph Cahill to become a three star admiral as the next commander of Naval Surface Forces/commander, Naval Surface Force, U.S. Pacific Fleet, also known as SWO Boss. Cahill currently served as commander of Naval Surface Force Atlantic (SURFLANT), a position he has held since 2023. Cahill's sea tours include serving as commander of the Arleigh Burke-class destroyer USS Preble (DDG-88), integrated air and missile warfare commander of Carrier Strike Group 9, and commander of Carrier Strike Group 15. If confirmed, Cahill will succeed current SWO Boss Vice Adm. Brendan McLane, who himself came into the position in late 2023 following his own stint leading SURFLANT.

RHIB Awards. Naval Sea Systems Command on May 1 awarded eight vendors contracts with a base value of $326 million for up to 474 composite rigid hull inflatable boats (RHIBs). The winners include ASIS Boats USA LLC (doing business as Ocean Craft Marine), Brig USA LLC (doing business as Fluid Marine Response), Ghostworks Marine Inc., Ribcraft USA LLC, St. Johns Ship Building Inc., Structural Composites Inc., United States Marine Inc., and The Whiskey Project Group USA LLC. The firm-fixed-price, indefinite-delivery, indefinite-quantity multiple award contracts, if exercised, plan to ultimately raise the value to $650 million over 10 years to all eight vendors combined. The first orders are expected to be finished by July 2026. The Navy said it received 15 offers.

T-ATS Start. Master Boat Builders, Inc. on May 5 announced the start of module fabrication for Navy Navajo-class Towing, Salvage, and Rescue Ship (T-ATS) program under a partnership with Austal USA. The company is building two T-ATS hull modules at its Coden, Ala., shipyard, which it highlighted is only 30 minutes away from Austal USA's shipyard where final erection and outfitting will occur. The company first announced its partnership to work under Austal USA in March, with hopes to gain experience working to Navy standards and production requirements for large-scale programs so it can later expand into more government shipbuilding programs.

LCU 1714. Austal USA on May 4 announced it started construction of its fifth Navy Landing Craft Utility (LCU) 1700-class vessel, LCU 1714, at its Mobile, Ala., shipyard on April 27. The Navy awarded the company a $91.5 million contract in 2023 to design and construct up to 12 of these vessels, with five construction contracts awarded so far. The LCUs are deployed from amphibious assault ships for heavy-lift capability from ship to shore and back. The first vessel in the program Austal built, LCU 1710, recently completed acceptance trials and the company noted it will soon be delivered to the Navy.

ABMS. L3Harris Technologies has been working on digital infrastructure for the Air Force's Advanced Battle Management System (ABMS) for the last several years, and the company now says it "has been selected to develop key features of the secure and resilient digital infrastructure" as the backbone of the system. "L3Harris will strengthen the [ABMS] data integration and networking capabilities," the company said. "This effort will be foundational to combining data from multiple sources and providing the warfighter with a real-time, comprehensive view of the battlefield." ABMS is a key piece of the Pentagon's future Combined Joint All-Domain Command and Control architecture. Kathy Crandall, the president of mission networks for L3Harris' pace and mission systems business, said in a company statement that ABMS' "new digital infrastructure will provide the warfighter with more secure, timely and usable information, allowing them to connect sensors and shooters across domains - in complex operational environments."

New U.S. Drone Maker. Orqa, the Croatian-based manufacturer of first person view (FPV) drones and a supplier to the Ukrainian military, has partnered with By Light Professional IT Services to begin manufacturing drones in the U.S. Orqa U.S. LLC will produce drones at By Light's 180,000 square foot facility in Port Orange, Fla., with full-rate production expected in October. Orqa's expansion to the U.S. is a result of the Defense Department's focus on expanding American-made drone production, supply chains and capacity through the Drone Dominance Program.

New InSar Provider. IonQ last week launched Inferometric Synthetic Aperture (InSAR) capabilities through its Capella Space business unit that operates radar-based satellites. IonQ said its InSAR capabilities enable "millimeter-precision ground deformation monitoring with fully automated tasking and data delivery, enabling customers to detect and track physical change on the Earth's surface consistently, at a frequency and scale never previously available from a commercial SAR provider." IonQ also said its solution offers automated tasking and frequent revisit rates based on customer needs.

Another Draper Flight. Ursa Major last week said the Air Force Research Laboratory recently conducted another flight of the Affordable Rapid Missile Demonstrator (ARMD) using the company's Draper liquid rocket engine. The ARMD flight came 45 days after a previous flight test, demonstrating a rapid turnaround and further expanding the flight envelop, Ursa said (Defense Daily, March 12). The company is under contract to the lab to characterize Draper in flight.

Space Raise. Astranis, which develops and manufactures satellites for high-Earth orbit, last week said it raised $455 million in new capital to accelerate production for commercial customers and scale up to support U.S. government programs of record as the Space Force looks to dramatically increase its budget. The funding includes a $300 million Series E round led by Snowpoint Ventures and Franklin Templeton, and a $155 million delayed-draw credit facility by Trinity Capital. San Francisco-based Astranis highlighted that it is a prime contractor for the initial phases of the Defense Department's Protected Tactical Satcom-Global, Resilient GPS and Andromeda programs.

...And Another. Scout Space last week said it raised $18 million in Series A funding expand its manufacturing and execute an upcoming mission. The Scout's Owl sensor will be on Blue Origin's Blue Ring maneuverable spacecraft for a Space Force space domain awareness mission. The funding round was led by Washington Harbour Partners.

ANELLO Funding Round. Navigation technology developer ANELLO Photonics raised $25 million in a Series B-2 round to accelerate the California-based company's production and product development. ANELLO has developed the Silicon Photonics Optical Gyroscope for precision navigation in GPS-denied environments in land, air and sea domains. The funding round was led by MESH and included Washington Harbour Partners, a new investor, and existing investors such as Lockheed Martin Ventures.

Space Acquisition. Rocket Lab plans to acquire Motiv Space Systems in a deal that further vertically integrates the company with motion control systems, precision mechanisms and solar drive assemblies for spacecraft. The acquisition will give Rocket Lab the in-house component supply to allow it to scale satellite constellation manufacturing. The pending deal, expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, will also give Rocket Lab robotics technology proven on Mars. Motiv, which is also based in California, has 50 employees.

Kodiak and GD. Kodiak AI and General Dynamics Land Systems on May 7 announced a new strategic collaboration to work on autonomous ground vehicles, with plans to pursue both U.S. Army and international opportunities. "Through this collaboration, Kodiak will provide the Kodiak Driver, its Physical AI-powered virtual driver that is operating driverlessly today, demonstrating the ability to scale autonomous operations beyond controlled testing. General Dynamics Land Systems will lead vehicle integration, power systems, communications and ruggedized platform development," Kodiak said in a statement. "Their collaboration establishes a scalable autonomy capability that can be applied across multiple military vehicle platforms and mission sets." Kodiak noted the partnership builds on its recent work with GD Land Systems on the Leonidas Autonomous Ground Vehicle, which was equipped with Epirus' Leonidas high-power microwave counter-drone capability.

Corporate News. Autonomous vessel developer and manufacturer Saronic Technologies last week said it has produced its 300 Corsair, a 24-foot speed boat unveiled in the fall of 2024 that entered prototyping a year ago and is now in serial production. Corsair has a range of 1,000 nautical miles and a top speed exceeding 35 knots and can deliver kinetic and non-kinetic effects. Kratos Defense & Security Solutions has selected Odon, Ind., for the future home of its arc jet and laser facility for hypersonic materials development. The site selection follows a potential $68 million award last fall through the Pentagon's Industrial Base Analysis and Sustainment program to design and build a moder mid-tier arc jet and coupled fiber laser facility for hypersonic materials evaluation, an effort Kratos calls Project Helios. Radio frequency technology company Hidden Level last week unveiled a new anechoic chamber at its manufacturing campus in Syracuse, N.Y., to allow it to accelerate development and production of solutions for airspace security and countering drones.

People News. Gabe Camarillo, under secretary of the Army during the Biden administration, has been appointed by Peraton as president of its Defense sector. Prior to joining Peraton, Camarillo was senior vice president of KBR's Defense Technology Solutions business unit. Drone interceptor developer Powerus last wee named Rear Adm. Milton Sands III to its advisory board. Sands is a former commander of the Naval Special Warfare Command.

results.title: Gustavo de Arístegui: Geopolitical Analysis for 4 May
results.body:
Persistent global energy crisis: 'Project Freedom' as a decisive variable

Monday 4 May begins under the unmistakable banner of 'Project Freedom', the military operation announced by the White House to escort merchant ships through the Strait of Hormuz, where, according to data from the British Royal Navy, nearly 20,000 sailors of all nationalities are currently stranded.

The initiative -- which President Trump has presented as a "humanitarian gesture" and which, at the same time, must be interpreted as a major strategic show of force -- marks the start of a new, more exposed and volatile phase of the confrontation following Operation Epic Fury and the ceasefire declared on 7 April.

In parallel, the US administration has confirmed the withdrawal of 5,000 troops from Germany, with an additional announcement of "much greater" cuts and specific warnings regarding Italy and Spain. The transatlantic rift, which I have been describing for months as a contained systemic fracture, is now entering a phase of public visibility that no spokesperson can any longer conceal.

Ukraine, for its part, is intensifying its strategic depth campaign against Russian refineries and logistics hubs, demonstrating an offensive capability that would have been unthinkable two years ago. And on the Indo-Pacific front, with the Balikatan 2026 exercises in full swing and the first Japanese presence of combat troops in the Philippines since the Second World War, it is becoming clear that the paradox of Iran's decapitation has not altered the underlying dynamics of Sino-American competition, but has instead accelerated them.

Facts

President Donald Trump announced on Sunday 3 May, via a post on Truth Social, the imminent launch of the operation dubbed "Project Freedom", aimed at guiding merchant ships that have been blocked for over two months through the Strait of Hormuz. US Central Command (CENTCOM) specified that the operation will deploy Arleigh Burke-class destroyers equipped with guided missiles, over 100 land-based and ship-based aircraft, multi-domain unmanned platforms and approximately 15,000 personnel.

The British Royal Navy estimates that naval traffic through the strait has fallen by more than ninety per cent since the start of the conflict and warns of a looming humanitarian crisis for the trapped seafarers. Iranian MP Ebrahim Azizi, a prominent member of the security establishment in Tehran, has warned that any US interference will be considered a violation of the current ceasefire.

Implications

The operation marks the first open and pre-announced US military deployment in the waters of the Strait since the declared suspension of hostilities, and upsets the delicate diplomatic balance that had sustained negotiations through the Pakistani intermediary.

The dictatorial and mafia-like oligarchy that continues to rule in Tehran -- with the Revolutionary Guard triumvirate comprising Generals Vahidi, Zolghadr and Rezaei in positions of effective control -- faces a classic dilemma: to yield and show weakness to its own base, or to respond and risk a second phase of the conflict which, this time, could prove fatal. For the Gulf allies, particularly Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Kuwait, the operation is a lifeline: the risk of a widespread force majeure declaration by QatarEnergy and the major Saudi producers had reached proportions that no budgetary calculation could absorb.

Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell confirmed on Friday 1 May that Secretary of War Pete Hegseth has ordered the withdrawal of approximately 5,000 US troops from Germany within six to twelve months. President Trump added on Saturday, speaking to the press before boarding Air Force One, that the cuts would go "far beyond" that initial figure.

The decision, which was not previously communicated to NATO's Allied Command, according to Alliance sources cited by Euronews, comes amid a public dispute between Washington and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, who stated that the United States was being "humiliated" by the prolongation of the Iranian conflict - a remark which, although considered imprudent and foolish, is unworthy of a statesman to utter in public. Trump has also stated that he is considering equivalent cuts in Italy and Spain, specifically mentioning both countries for their "unhelpful response" to the conflict.

The Republican chairmen of the Armed Services Committees, Senator Roger Wicker and Congressman Mike Rogers, expressed "deep concern" over the decision, warning that it sends "the wrong signal to Vladimir Putin".

Implications

We are witnessing the first visible structural shift in a reorganisation of US military posture in Europe that has been in the making for years. The move is, above all, political: Trump is punishing the "public disloyalty" of allies who have refused to join the military effort against Iran and who, in Germany's case, have added the comparative insult of open criticism. He said he was disappointed with Meloni and that the Spanish government had been "horrible".

For Spain, the situation is particularly delicate and, to put it with the frankness the moment demands, alarming. The Spanish government's attitude, which borders on irresponsibility and has shown itself to be more uncomfortable with Washington than with Tehran, stands in stark contrast to the operational reality of the Rota and Morón bases, which have functioned and continue to function as active logistical support for Operation Epic Fury. This inconsistency between the government's public rhetoric and the military reality at the bases cannot be sustained indefinitely without cost, and that cost is beginning to materialise in the form of an open bill.

Outlooks and scenarios

The withdrawal from Germany, though limited in its initial numerical scope, opens the door to a broader pullback that will affect the forward deployment in Central and Eastern Europe. Senate Republicans are rightly calling for troops not to leave Europe, but to be redeployed eastwards, where allies such as Poland and Romania have done their homework in terms of investment and access.

For Spain, the realistic scenario is as follows: if Madrid persists with its current line, a significant reduction in the US presence at Rota or Morón cannot be ruled out, which would deprive the country not only of a first-rate geopolitical asset but also of a not inconsiderable volume of investment and employment. Spanish foreign policy stands at a crossroads that leaves no room for the usual rhetorical posturing.

And it is worth recalling, once again, that the democratic transition - the greatest achievement of contemporary Spain and of King Juan Carlos I as its architect - placed the country at the heart of the Atlantic system precisely because its leaders from the UCD, Felipe González's PSOE and the PP understood that active neutrality towards the adversary is, in the blunt language of geopolitics, alignment with the enemy.

Facts

Between 2 and 3 May, the Ukrainian Defence Forces maintained a sustained campaign against Russian logistics and energy hubs. The Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) confirmed the attack on the Transneft pumping station in the city of Perm, more than 1,600 kilometres from the border, described by the agency itself as a "strategically important hub of the main oil transport system".

The Ukrainian General Staff reported 141 engagements in the past 24 hours, with particular pressure in the Pokrovsk, Huliaipole and Kostiantynivka sectors. Ukrainian air defences neutralised 175 Russian drones and five missiles on 3 May. Meanwhile, Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk denounced a "disintegration" of NATO following the announcement of the US withdrawal from Germany.

Implications

Ukraine is demonstrating a level of technological and operational maturity -- which the Institute for the Study of War in Washington describes as a sustained increase in scope, volume and intensity -- that is rewriting the terms of the conflict. Kyiv no longer aspires merely to territorial defence: the systematic campaign against Russian refineries, oil pipelines and export ports aims to undermine the economic basis of Moscow's war effort.

In March, Reuters estimated the loss of Russian export capacity at around forty per cent; Zelensky raised the estimate of revenue losses to $2.3 billion per month. This is, to put it bluntly, variable-temperature warfare in its most sophisticated form: the attacker sets the pace and the attacked pays the energy bill in each of its markets.

Outlook and scenarios

The Ukrainian campaign of strategic depth will continue to escalate if Kyiv manages to maintain the flow of long-range technologies, some of which are home-grown -- Minister Fedorov has stated that twenty per cent of defence resources are allocated to innovation. The main short-term risk is the delay announced by the United States in arms shipments to Europe, according to the Financial Times, which could indirectly affect Ukraine.

Putin faces the dilemma of escalating vertically -- with the risk of tactical nuclear weapons ever-present as an ambiguous threat -- or conceding on the political front. And conceding, in his logic of personalist power, amounts to internal defeat. We maintain an unequivocal editorial line: Russian aggression against Ukraine runs counter to the rules-based international order and the fundamental principle that force cannot serve as a legitimate means of territorial acquisition.

Facts

The Tasnim news agency, linked to the Iranian Revolutionary Guard, reported on Saturday that Tehran has submitted to the US government, via a Pakistani intermediary, a fourteen-point response to Washington's previous proposal. The Iranian offer calls for "an end to the war on all fronts, including Lebanon", the release of frozen assets, the lifting of sanctions and a "new mechanism for the Strait of Hormuz".

Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi stated that the proposal seeks "a permanent end" to the war. Trump responded that he "cannot imagine" the proposal being acceptable, whilst the Iranian Parliament is pushing forward a twelve-point bill that would ban the passage of Israeli ships and demand war reparations from ships of "hostile countries". The Trump Administration has, in parallel, accelerated arms sales to Israel, Qatar, the Emirates and Kuwait worth over $8 billion.

Implications

The gulf between the two sides remains vast. Tehran is playing for time, aware that domestic pressure on the US economy -- petrol prices above four dollars a gallon, according to figures from late March verified by ABC News, and resurgent inflation -- could alter Washington's negotiating position.

This is the classic tactic of Tehran's dictatorial and mafia-like oligarchy: to buy time whilst reorganising the terrorist apparatus, reactivating Hezbollah networks in Lebanon and Syria, Houthi terrorists in Yemen, pro-Iranian terrorist militias in Iraq, and Hamas terrorists in Gaza. The parallel acceleration of US arms sales to Gulf allies is a consistent signal: Washington is preparing so that, should diplomacy fail, its partners are not left defenceless.

Outlook and scenarios

The probability of a breakdown in negotiations over the next two or three weeks is, in this analyst's view, substantially higher than that of an agreement. The Iranian regime needs time -- to rebuild capabilities, to consolidate the internal military triumvirate following the removal of Ali Khamenei, and to oust President Pezeshkian, who is being systematically blocked -- whilst Trump, by contrast, needs visible results before the mid-term elections. This temporal asymmetry is, in itself, a source of friction.

The "paradox of decapitation" persists: without a single interlocutor with full authority in Tehran, every Iranian concession risks being undermined by the faction that feels it has been excluded from the equation. The regime remains, it must be remembered, cosmically corrupt, ruthless and brutal, and any agreement without a coherent plan for the "day after" is, quite simply, disaster management.

Facts

The Balikatan 2026 combined exercises, taking place between 20 April and 8 May, bring together some 17,000 troops from the Philippines, the United States, Japan, Australia, New Zealand, Canada and France. For the first time since the Second World War, Japan has deployed around 1,400 combat troops to Philippine soil, under the reciprocal access agreement in force since last year.

The Chinese People's Liberation Army Southern Theatre Command announced on 24 April that a surface task force comprising Type 055 and 052D destroyers, a Type 054A frigate and a Type 903A logistics vessel was conducting manoeuvres east of the Luzon Strait. The aircraft carrier Liaoning transited the Taiwan Strait southwards on 20 April. According to the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, Beijing has issued airspace restrictions lasting up to forty days -- far exceeding the usual duration of an exercise -- without official justification, and the Chinese fishing fleet has formed organised groups stretching over two hundred kilometres in the East China Sea.

Implications

China is taking advantage of the US's polarised focus on the Iranian theatre to accelerate its strategic positioning in the first island chain of the Pacific. The presence of Japanese combat troops in the Philippines is a historic milestone marking the symbolic closure of a seventy-five-year cycle, which Beijing has denounced in particularly harsh terms, speaking of a "resurgence of militarism". Chinese expansionism -- in the South China Sea, the Taiwan Strait, the Pacific towards the Maldives and Sri Lanka, and its growing control of rare earth supply chains -- demands a level of vigilance that Europe, preoccupied with its internal debates, continues to fail to provide.

Outlook and scenarios

The 2026-2027 window is critical. The Pentagon has warned that China aims to be in a position to win a war over Taiwan by the end of 2027. The "Taiwanisation" of the Indo-Pacific, with the first island chain activated and Tokyo fully committed, raises the opportunity cost of any military adventure by Beijing, but at the same time reduces the margins for error. The summit planned between Xi Jinping and Trump in April, according to analysts at Wellington Management, should produce an interim agreement, but long-term strategic decoupling -- particularly in emerging technologies, defence and critical minerals -- is already irreversible.

Facts

The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, in place since 28 February, has caused what the International Energy Agency describes as "the greatest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market". Brent crude is hovering around eighty-two dollars a barrel, with projections from Goldman Sachs and Barclays putting the price at one hundred or one hundred and fifty dollars in the event of a prolonged blockade. Petrol in the United States exceeded four dollars a gallon at the end of March.

QatarEnergy invoked force majeure on its liquefied natural gas contracts. Spirit Airlines ceased operations on 2 May, citing fuel costs. War insurance premiums for transit through the Strait have tripled or quadrupled. CENTCOM reports that the US Navy has forced the return of forty-eight Iranian vessels in the last twenty days as part of the dual blockade of Iranian ports in place since 13 April.

Implications

'Project Freedom', beyond its military component, is the Trump administration's decisive economic gamble to prevent resurgent inflation from eroding its political capital ahead of the mid-term elections. Paradoxically, the United States is benefiting in part from the shock: US exports of crude oil and derivatives reached almost 12.9 million barrels per day in April, and Uncle Sam is repositioning itself as an "indispensable supplier", a formulation aptly captured by Geopolitical Monitor.

But the importing economies -- Asia receives 75 per cent of the crude oil and 59 per cent of the LNG from the Strait, with China, India, Japan and South Korea as the main recipients -- are suffering a blow that is reshaping strategic alliances in real time. OPEC+ has committed to an increase of 206,000 barrels per day to mitigate the shortfall, a figure that is clearly insufficient.

Outlooks and scenarios

If 'Project Freedom' manages to reopen the shipping lane without major incidents over the next two or three weeks, crude oil prices could correct by between 10 and 15 per cent within days, and the stagflation scenario that some economists have been warning of -- with an inevitable echo of the 1970s -- will be postponed.

If, on the other hand, a significant incident occurs -- a vessel struck, an escort vessel attacked, a mine triggered -- the Brent price curve could soar within hours towards $100 and, in the worst-case scenario, towards $150. For Europe, the outlook is precarious: its decades-long dependence and strategic short-sightedness have been laid bare, and it lacks the levers to influence the course of events. The "contained systemic fracture" of the Atlantic order is also manifesting itself, and painfully so, in this matter.

Coverage over the last twenty-four hours presents narrative lines that converge on the facts but diverge sharply in their interpretation, which is in itself a significant point.

CNN, CBS News and NBC have prioritised minute-by-minute coverage of "Project Freedom" in the Strait of Hormuz, emphasising the humanitarian dimension -- the 20,000 stranded sailors -- and treating the military implications with caution. The coverage has included Isobel Yeung's (CNN) interview with a stranded captain, an effective narrative device.

The New York Times and The Washington Post maintain an analytical line concerned with the lack of post-conflict planning, highlighting critical voices within the Republican Party (Wicker, Rogers) regarding the withdrawal of troops from Germany.

The Wall Street Journal and the Financial Times have focused on the macroeconomic implications: oil prices, war insurance premiums, QatarEnergy's declaration of force majeure, and the announced delay in arms shipments to Europe.

The Times of London and The Telegraph have prioritised the dual blockade aspect and the role of the Royal Navy, with editorial coverage favourable to the US operation but critical of the lack of allied coordination.

The Guardian maintains a more sceptical line regarding "Project Freedom", emphasising the risk of escalation and Iranian warnings.

Le Monde, Le Figaro and Libération focus their attention on the deterioration of transatlantic relations, with particular analysis of Friedrich Merz's shift in Germany's stance -- from firm initial alliance to open criticism -- and cautious references to the Spanish position.

FAZ and Die Welt offer, as might be expected, the most detailed coverage of the Pentagon's announcement regarding the troop withdrawal, with a tone of institutional gravity. Die Zeit provides editorial analysis denouncing the lack of prior coordination.

Corriere della Sera and La Repubblica highlight Trump's explicit mention of Italy among the countries subject to possible further cuts. L'Osservatore Romano maintains its traditional call for dialogue.

The BBC and France Info provide balanced factual coverage with an emphasis on the humanitarian consequences in Lebanon -- seventy-three dead and one hundred and sixty-three injured since 30 April, according to the Lebanese Ministry of Health.

Fox News has openly backed "Project Freedom" and the withdrawal of troops from Germany as an application of the "America First" principle.

Al Jazeera, Al Arabiya and Al-Hayat provide extensive coverage of the stance of the Iranian dictatorial oligarchy and the fourteen-point proposal. Al-Quds Al-Arabi and Al Hayat Al Jadida prioritise the ceasefire in Lebanon and reports of mutual violations.

Yedioth Ahronoth, Israel Hayom and the Jerusalem Post are following in detail US arms sales to Israel, Qatar, the UAE and Kuwait -- worth over $8 billion -- and Israeli actions in southern Lebanon.

Haaretz takes a more nuanced line, featuring critical voices within the Israeli establishment regarding the sustainability of the operation.

Asharq Al-Awsat, Arab News and Al Riyadh report on Saudi relief regarding 'Project Freedom' and highlight the role of Arab ships unable to transit.

The Times of India, Hindustan Times and Indian Express are following the Strait crisis with clear geo-economic interest -- India receives forty per cent of its crude oil via Hormuz -- and the Balikatan 2026 exercises, in which New Delhi is closely observing Japan's positioning.

The South China Morning Post and China Daily harshly criticise Tokyo for its "offensive and expansionist stance" and reinforce the narrative of Japanese "new militarism". There is extensive coverage of the PLA's manoeuvres in the Luzon Strait.

Yomiuri Shimbun and the Tokyo Times highlight the historic nature of the deployment of Japanese troops to the Philippines and how it fits into the revised national security doctrine under Prime Minister Takaichi.

Russia Today, TASS and Vesti maintain their usual propaganda line, describing Ukrainian attacks on refineries as "terrorism" and amplifying the Kremlin's voice on the "disintegration" of NATO.

Ukrainian Pravda, UKRINFORM, Kyiv Post and The Kyiv Independent provide detailed coverage of the fighting -- 141 engagements in the last 24 hours -- and the strategic depth campaign against Transneft in Perm. The Kyiv Independent reports on Polish Prime Minister Tusk's condemnation of NATO's "disintegration".

Foreign Affairs, The Economist and The National Interest publish in-depth analyses: Geopolitical Monitor has argued that "if the Iranian regime survives, what comes next is not a choice between proxies or nuclear weapons; it is the recognition that nuclear weapons are the only rational security option". A disturbing read that deserves careful consideration.

RUSI, IISS, CSIS and IFRI agree on the analysis regarding the urgent need for an autonomous European defence architecture. The Economist Intelligence Unit has raised its global inflation forecasts for the second half of the year.

Politico, Axios, The Hill and Bloomberg are focusing their coverage on the internal dynamics in Washington, with Republicans on the Armed Services Committee expressing concern, Democrats accusing Hegseth of misleading the public about the progress of the war, and growing friction with Mike Johnson on Capitol Hill.

The events of 4 May confirm a thesis I have been maintaining since the start of Operation Epic Fury and which deserves, once again, to be clearly stated: the oligarchic-jihadist regime in Tehran -- dictatorial, mafia-like, cosmically corrupt, ruthless and brutal -- can be militarily wounded and decapitated, but it will not be defeated unless there is a coherent and executable plan for the 'day after'. The Trump Administration has demonstrated remarkable kinetic capability and a determination that was beyond the reach of the previous Administration; what it has not yet demonstrated is that it possesses a comprehensive strategic architecture linking tactical success with political transformation. And in geopolitics, tactical successes that are not capitalised upon invariably end up, with monotonous regularity, turning into strategic defeats in the long run.

"Project Freedom" is, it must be stated clearly, a necessary operation. 20,000 trapped sailors constitute a humanitarian disgrace that no international system worthy of the name can tolerate for any longer than the weeks that have already been tolerated. Freedom of navigation is, moreover, the foundation upon which the entire global economic architecture has rested since 1945, and abandoning that foundation amounts to accepting a world order dictated by the dictatorial oligarchy in Tehran and its terrorist proxies. The undersigned is therefore firmly in favour of the operation. But a word of warning is in order: if Washington does not accompany the military force with a coherent geostrategic plan, 'Project Freedom' will be a military victory with spectacular media coverage but extraordinarily limited strategic returns.

Regarding the withdrawal of troops from Germany and the threats to Italy and Spain, this analyst must reiterate what he has been writing in La Razón and El Debate over the past few months: the Spanish Government's stance, which is dangerously verging on irresponsibility and has shown itself to be more hostile towards Washington than towards Tehran, is simply untenable. The bases at Rota and Morón function as active logistical support for Operation Epic Fury -- that is the verifiable military reality -- and the government's public discourse is manifestly inconsistent with that operational reality. That inconsistency comes at a price that is already beginning to be paid, and which will be paid dearly if the course is not corrected. Europe, once again, finds itself incapable of taking its own defence, its own security and its own destiny seriously. The Europe of the 21st century remains, in essence, the strategically short-sighted Europe that I have been describing in my forthcoming book on the continent's 10 geostrategic deadly sins.

On Ukraine, it is worth reiterating the unambiguous editorial line: Russian aggression runs counter to the fundamental principles of the international order, and the use of force to acquire territory is unacceptable, whether carried out by Moscow, Beijing or any other actor. The negotiations the Trump Administration is pursuing with Russia deserve the critical and demanding scrutiny that great principles demand: peace that rewards aggression is not peace; it is an invitation to the next aggression.

On the Indo-Pacific front, the presence of Japanese combat troops in the Philippines for the first time since 1945 is a historic event whose magnitude has not yet been fully grasped. Tokyo is closing one chapter and opening another. Chinese expansionism, as patient as it is systematic, now faces coordinated resistance that alters its cost-benefit calculations. But coordination, once established, must be sustained; the allies of the first island chain require strategic certainties that only Washington can offer and which, for the reasons outlined regarding Germany, are not entirely evident today.

I conclude with the formulation that best sums up the moment. We are in a war of varying intensity which, in the case of Russia and the Iranian dictatorship, clearly falls squarely within the realm of 'hybrid warfare'. Success in this type of complex, multifaceted conflict, unfolding across different layers and intensities, is not measured by a single victory but by the ability to stay the course through successive phases of tension.

Western leadership requires, at this moment, virtues that the mediocre and short-sighted political class of the 21st century painfully lacks: historical vision, doctrinal clarity and clarity regarding the values that inspire our democracies, firmness in the defence of our security and interests, and an unwavering determination in their implementation. Without these pillars, the international order will continue to erode to the point where restoring it will cost, as is often the case in such matters, an order of magnitude more than it would have cost to preserve it in time.

results.title: The Iran war has the world buying more clean energy. China stands to benefit the most | CNN Business
results.body:
The war in Iran has sent oil-starved countries scrambling for fuel. Many are opting for energy alternatives -- and turning to the renewables king of the planet: China.

Chinese exports of solar technology, batteries and electric vehicles all reached record highs in March, according to energy think tank Ember, a sign that the historic oil supply shock is accelerating the adoption of clean energy around the world.

After the US and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran in late February, the Iranian military effectively barricaded the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off about one-fifth of global oil and natural gas supply. Oil price volatility has surged as the conflict has expanded into the Middle East and negotiations to end the war have stalled.

Workers carry solar panels to install them at a solar farm in the desert, in Lingwu, Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region, China, on April 14, 2025.

China Daily/Reuters

Meanwhile, Asian nations that depend on the Middle East for energy imports are trying to mitigate fuel shortages by encouraging energy conservation and shortening work hours. As countries invest more in renewable energy, China stands to benefit as the world's largest manufacturer of electric vehicles, wind turbines and solar panels.

A Thursday report from Ember said China exported 68 gigawatts of solar technology in March, surpassing the previous record set in August by 50%. Fifty countries set new records for Chinese solar imports, with the most significant growth coming from emerging markets in Asia and Africa hit hardest by the energy crisis, according to the think tank.

"Fossil shocks are boosting the solar surge," said Euan Graham, senior analyst at Ember, in the report. "Solar has already become the engine of the global economy, and now the current fossil fuel price shocks are taking it up a gear."

Ember said exports of solar, batteries and EVs in total rose 70% in March year over year, according to Chinese customs data. Those categories have become known in China as the "new three," contributing significantly to the country's GDP in place of clothing, home appliances and furniture exports that previously drove growth.

China's battery exports reached $10 billion in March, with particularly high growth rates in the European Union, Australia and India, Ember said.

Paradigm shifts

Arleigh Burke-class guided-missile destroyer USS Rafael Peralta patrols the Arabian Sea during a maritime blockade against ships entering or exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas, April 15.

US Navy

Uncertainty over when the Strait of Hormuz will reopen has spurred deeper regional anxieties about energy security, helping to hasten the transition to clean energy, analysts said.

The US and Iran have agreed to a ceasefire while they negotiate terms to end the war, but tensions in the strait have remained elevated. Both US and Iranian forces have seized ships in the critical passageway, subduing further attempts to transit through.

The oil crisis has also reshuffled regional trade and relationships as nations seek to insulate themselves from the supply shock. Building out renewable capacity has been one way to cushion the blow.

"As we face the second fossil fuel shock in less than 5 years, the lesson for our country is clear: The era of fossil fuel security is over, and the era of clean energy security must come of age," said UK Energy Secretary Ed Miliband, in a statement this week on the need to reduce the nation's reliance on gas for electricity.

In China, massive state investment in green energy industries has bolstered its energy self-sufficiency, reducing its exposure to the oil shortage. Its dominance in renewable technology has also granted the country more geopolitical and economic influence as it exports its technology.

Pakistan has been spared some of the impact from the war, since it began drastically importing cheap Chinese solar panels a few years ago. Using solar energy rather than costly oil imports is estimated to save the country billions of dollars each year.

A robotic arm works on photovoltaic modules at the workshop of Alternative Energy Solar Co., Ltd. on December 17, 2025, in Huaian, Jiangsu Province of China.

VCG/Visual China Group/VCG via Getty Images

"China has been regarded as a low-cost supplier, but is increasingly treated as a long-term partner in the energy transition," wrote Jeong Won Kim, a senior research fellow at the Energy Studies Institute at the National University of Singapore.

And it's not just solar panels. Ember analysts estimated that global EV adoption had reduced oil consumption by about 1.7 million barrels last year -- and as oil prices rose at the start of the Middle East conflict, Chinese state media reported that the country's EV giants had seen an overseas sales surge.

According to the China Passenger Car Association, Chinese exports of electric vehicles and hybrids hit a record high in March, increasing 140% compared with the same period a year ago.

Analysts said part of the surge in solar sales last month was due to stockpiling before China discontinued a tax rebate in April. Lauri Myllyvirta, co-founder of the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air, said it's unlikely the significant increase in exports from March will be sustainable.

Still, the conflict in the Middle East has strengthened the long-term case for alternative energy, he added.

"The fall in the costs of solar power and batteries, and now the higher and more volatile fossil fuel prices have made solar a no-brainer for a large share of global electricity consumers," he said.

Ember analysts estimated that global EV adoption had reduced oil consumption by about 1.7 million barrels last year -- and as oil prices rose at the start of the Middle East conflict, Chinese state media reported that the country's EV giants had seen an overseas sales surge.

According to the China Passenger Car Association, Chinese exports of electric vehicles and hybrids hit a record high in March, increasing 140% compared with the same period a year ago.

Analysts said part of the surge in solar sales last month was due to stockpiling before China discontinued a tax rebate in April. Lauri Myllyvirta, co-founder of the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air, said it's unlikely the significant increase in exports from March will be sustainable.

Still, the conflict in the Middle East has strengthened the long-term case for alternative energy, he added.

"The fall in the costs of solar power and batteries, and now the higher and more volatile fossil fuel prices have made solar a no-brainer for a large share of global electricity consumers," he said.
